name: JBrowse Navigator
description: Learn to navigate a genome browser through guided exploration

awards.name: First Steps
awards.flavorText: You moved! The genome is yours to explore.

awards.name: Eagle Eye
awards.flavorText: Individual nucleotides. You're reading the code of life.

awards.name: Collector
awards.flavorText: Multiple data types, one view. Serious analysis.

awards.name: Biologist
awards.flavorText: You speak the language of genomes.

tasks.title: Zoom In
tasks.description: Use your scroll wheel (or pinch gesture) on the genome view to zoom in.
hints: Place your mouse cursor over the colored bar and scroll up

coaching.message: Place your mouse over the genome display and scroll your mouse wheel up

tasks.title: Look Around
tasks.description: Click and drag the genome view to slide left or right.
hints: Click and hold, then drag sideways

coaching.message: Click and hold on the genome display, then drag left or right

tasks.title: Read the Code
tasks.description: Zoom in until you can see individual letters (A, T, G, C) in the sequence track. These are the nucleotide bases — the building blocks of DNA.
hints: Keep scrolling to zoom in further
The letters appear when you're zoomed in enough — they represent adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine

tasks.title: Reveal Hidden Data
tasks.description: The genome has more data than what's shown. Open the track selector and turn on the 'volvox-sorted-vcf' track (variants data).
hints: Look for 'Open track selector' at the bottom of the genome view
Search for 'vcf' in the track list and click its checkbox

coaching.message: Look for a button or '+' icon near the bottom of the genome view to open the track selector

tasks.title: Name That Feature
tasks.description: Pan along ctgA until you find an annotated feature (a colored block with a label). Zoom in if needed to read its name. Type the name below.
hints: Click and drag to pan along the genome
Zoom in with the scroll wheel to see feature labels
Features have names like 'EDEN', 'Apple1', 'seg01', etc.

tasks.title: The Other Contig
tasks.description: This genome has two contigs (chromosome-like sequences): ctgA and ctgB. Navigate to ctgB.
hints: Try typing 'ctgB' in the search bar at the top of the genome view
ctgB is much shorter than ctgA — only about 6,000 base pairs

tasks.title: Gene Anatomy
tasks.description: Navigate back to ctgA and find the gene called 'EDEN'. Zoom in to see its internal structure. The thick blocks are exons (protein-coding regions). How many exons does EDEN have?
hints: Search for 'EDEN' in the search bar
Exons are the thick colored blocks; thin lines between them are introns
Count each thick block separately

tasks.title: The Neighbor
tasks.description: Starting at gene EDEN, pan to the right. What is the name of the next annotated feature you find?
hints: Navigate to EDEN first, then click-drag to pan rightward
Zoom in if you need to read the label

tasks.title: Variant Spotting
tasks.description: With the VCF variants track visible, navigate to ctgA around position 5,000. Click on a variant feature to inspect it. What type of variant is it?
hints: Navigate to ctgA:4000-6000 and look for variant markers
Click on a variant to open its detail widget
The variant type might be listed as the REF and ALT alleles

tasks.title: Quick Check
tasks.description: What are the two contigs in this genome?

tasks.title: Layers of Evidence
tasks.description: Open at least 3 tracks simultaneously (e.g., genes, variants, and an alignments track). Find a region where data from different tracks tells an interesting story. Describe what you see in 1-2 sentences.
hints: Open the track selector and add more tracks
Try the alignments track alongside genes and variants
Look for regions where variant calls overlap with gene features

tasks.title: Free Exploration
tasks.description: Spend a minute freely exploring the genome. Navigate anywhere, open any tracks. When done, describe the most interesting thing you found.
hints: Try zooming to different scales — overview, gene level, base pair level
Open tracks you haven't tried yet
Look for patterns: coverage dips, variant clusters, gene-dense regions

tasks.title: Teach a Robot
tasks.description: Navigate to a region you find interesting. Describe step-by-step how you got there and what makes it worth looking at. (Your description will help train an AI navigation agent.)
hints: Think about what actions you took: zoom, pan, open tracks
Explain why you chose to look where you did

tasks.title: Graduation
tasks.description: Final question: what would you want to explore further in this genome, or in a larger genome, if you had more time?
